context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on progress, modernization, development, and planning, and I’m kinda stuck on how to separate those terms. I think they’re connected, but rn they all blur together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — recognizing that the terms are connected but distinct is actually the core challenge of this prompt. Let's untangle them one at a time. Pick one of the four terms — progress, modernization, development, or planning — and tell me in your own words what you think it means. Don't worry about being precise yet, just go with your gut.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of their thinking process and has identified a real conceptual challenge. Rather than explaining the differences between the terms, I should use a Socratic question to get them to start reasoning on their own. I'll ask them to pick one term and describe what it means to them, which will naturally open up the distinctions through their own reflection.

exchanges.student: I think progress means society getting “better” over time, but idk better for who or by whose standard. That part is what’s confusing me tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you've identified — 'better for whom?' — is actually central to the whole assignment. Now push that thought further: if 'better' depends on who's defining it, what does that suggest about whether progress is a universal concept or something more specific to a particular culture or historical moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown genuine insight by questioning the normative dimension of 'progress' — who defines 'better' and by whose standard. This is actually a sophisticated observation that connects directly to the prompt's question about whether progress is a Western notion. I should affirm this without flattering excessively, and then push them to develop this line of thinking further with a Socratic question that moves toward the Western/non-Western dimension of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I guess that makes it seem not fully universal, since diff societies might define “better” in diff ways. But I’m not sure if that means progress itself is Western, or just the Enlightenment version of it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction. So how might you test it? Can you think of any pre-Enlightenment or non-Western societies that had their own ideas about society improving or moving toward a better state — even if they didn't use the word 'progress'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a really important distinction between 'progress as a concept' and 'a specific Enlightenment version of progress.' This directly addresses the prompt's question about whether progress emerged during the Enlightenment or can be traced to earlier/non-Western formations. I should push them to think about historical and non-Western examples to test their hypothesis, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe like Confucian ideas about good order or Islamic political thought about just rule? I’m kinda guessing tho, and idk if that counts as “progress” or just stability.
exchanges.tutor: Those are reasonable examples to explore — don't dismiss them. Now think about this: what is it about the Enlightenment idea of progress that might make it different from ideas of good order or just rule? Is there something about its relationship to time or direction that sets it apart?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is onto something real but is second-guessing themselves. The examples they've given are legitimate and worth pursuing. The distinction they're drawing between 'progress' and 'stability' is actually a productive one that can help them articulate what makes the Western Enlightenment notion of progress distinctive (linearity, forward movement, improvement over time) versus other conceptions. I should validate the direction without confirming the answer, and ask them to think about what specifically distinguishes those non-Western concepts from the Enlightenment version.

exchanges.student: I think Enlightenment progress is more linear, like history moves forward and humans can improve society through reason/science. The other ideas might be more about maintaining moral order, not necessarily constant forward change.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong distinction. Now connect it to the other terms: given what you just said about Enlightenment progress, how do you think 'modernization' relates to it? Is modernization the same thing, a subset, or something different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely strong conceptual move — identifying linearity and reason/science as distinctive features of Enlightenment progress versus cyclical or moral-order-based conceptions. This is solid thinking that they can build on in their paper. Now I should push them to connect this to the other terms in the prompt — modernization, development, and planning — since they said those blur together. The natural next step is to ask how modernization relates to this Enlightenment version of progress they've just described.

exchanges.student: I think modernization is like a more specific version of progress, maybe when “getting better” means becoming more industrial, bureaucratic, secular, or Western-style. So not exactly the same, but def connected.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're building a useful conceptual ladder here. So where does 'development' fit on that ladder? Is it closer to progress, closer to modernization, or something else entirely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a solid conceptual chain. Their observation that modernization implies a specific model (industrial, bureaucratic, secular, Western-style) is good and directly relevant to the prompt. Now I should push them toward 'development' and 'planning' to complete the conceptual map. The key move is to get them to see development as perhaps the policy/institutional operationalization of modernization, and planning as the tool/practice through which development is implemented. A single question should do it.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking development is broader policy language, like improving welfare/economy, but it often borrows the assumptions of modernization. Ngl I’m still fuzzy on how to separate those two clearly.
exchanges.tutor: Think about who uses each word and in what context — a World Bank economist, a political scientist, a government planner. Does that help sharpen the distinction for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but stuck on the development/modernization distinction. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to think about who uses each term and in what context — this institutional/actor-based angle should help them see the distinction more clearly without me giving it away. Also, we're about 7 exchanges in, and the student has built up enough conceptual material to start writing. I should help them make one more distinction and then push toward integration.

exchanges.student: Yeah, kinda — modernization sounds more like a theory of social change, while development is more the policy/practice goal people use in states or aid stuff. I think development can include modernization ideas without being identical to them.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid conceptual map you've built — progress, modernization, development, and planning each occupying a distinct but connected place. You now have enough to start writing. Try drafting a paragraph that lays out these distinctions in your own words. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now built a solid conceptual map across all four terms. They have enough material to start writing. Following the rule about integrating after ~3 messages on similar ideas, and given we're near the end of the planned conversation length, I should push them to now attempt a written synthesis rather than continuing to refine concepts verbally. I should not write anything for them, just direct them toward the writing task.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Although progress, modernization, development, and planning are often used as if they mean the same thing, they come from different historical traditions and carry different assumptions about how societies change.” Is that too generic?
exchanges.tutor: It's a functional opening, but read it again — does it actually tell the reader anything specific yet? Think about the distinctions you just worked out. What would make this sentence do more intellectual work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for feedback on their opening sentence. I should not write or suggest language for them, but I can evaluate it critically and push them to make it stronger by asking what's missing. The sentence is decent but vague — it doesn't yet signal the specific analytical distinctions they've actually worked out in our conversation. I should push them to think about what their sentence promises the reader and whether it delivers.

exchanges.student: Yeah true, it’s kinda vague rn. Maybe I should name the key claim more directly, like that progress isn’t just Western but modern development planning uses a specifically Enlightenment/modernist version of it?
exchanges.tutor: That's a much stronger claim — it's specific and arguable. One thing to keep in mind as you write: the prompt also asks about the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in development. Have you thought about where that fits in your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a much stronger and more specific thesis that directly addresses the prompt. This is genuine progress and worth acknowledging. Since we're at the end of the planned conversation length, I should affirm this direction, remind them of the full scope of the prompt (they haven't touched the tradition/modernity relationship yet), and encourage them to keep writing independently.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently prompting the student to generate ideas rather than supplying them. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the student arrived at the key conceptual distinctions and thesis claim independently. The dialogue progressed logically, responses were concise and well-targeted, and tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a final answer or near-submission-ready content. Every turn posed a question or invited the student to extend their own thinking. The student generated all key distinctions (linear vs. cyclical progress, modernization as a subset, development as policy language) independently. No rewriting of student text occurred; in Turn 10 the tutor simply affirmed the student's self-revised claim and redirected to an unaddressed prompt element. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress — acknowledging the 'better for whom' insight, the Confucian/Islamic examples, and the conceptual ladder metaphor — and scaffolded each step from the student's prior response. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback in Turn 9 ('does it actually tell the reader anything specific yet?') and guided the student toward a more argumentative claim, demonstrating method-level coaching. 2.1–2.2: No repetition without progress; the conversation advanced each turn. No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding went uncorrected. 3.1–3.2: Responses were consistently brief and focused; tone was warm and formative throughout.
